title: Frontier Fine-Tuning Lab
description: Run a focused fine-tuning workflow and compare it against prompt-only and retrieval-based alternatives so you can defend the decision to adapt a model.
whyItMatters: Fine-tuning is no longer the default move, which makes judgment around when to use it more valuable. This project teaches that judgment.
skillsLearned: Evaluation of fine-tuned vs base models
prerequisites: Complete the Image Classifier project (for training loop familiarity)

steps.title: Choose a fine-tuning task
steps.description: Pick a specific task where fine-tuning makes sense: email classification, code generation for a specific framework, domain-specific Q&A, or structured data extraction from unstructured text.
steps.tips: Fine-tuning works best when you need consistent output format or domain-specific knowledge, not general intelligence.

steps.title: Prepare training data
steps.description: Create or collect at least 100 training examples (ideally 500+). Each example should be an input/output pair in the format your task requires. Split into train/validation sets. Quality matters more than quantity.

steps.title: Choose a base model
steps.description: Select a model to fine-tune. For learning: Llama 3 8B or Mistral 7B (open-source, can run locally). For production: OpenAI fine-tuning API or Anthropic fine-tuning (easier but less control).

steps.title: Fine-tune with LoRA
steps.description: Use LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning — it trains only a small number of adapter parameters instead of the full model, dramatically reducing compute and memory requirements. Use the Hugging Face PEFT library.

steps.title: Evaluate against baseline
steps.description: Run both the base model (with prompting) and the fine-tuned model on a held-out test set. Compare accuracy, consistency, and output quality. Is fine-tuning actually better for this task?

steps.title: Iterate on data quality
steps.description: Look at examples where the fine-tuned model fails. Are the failures due to bad training data? Add more examples in the areas where the model struggles. Retrain and re-evaluate.

steps.title: Document the decision
steps.description: Write a brief report: task description, why fine-tuning was chosen over prompting, data preparation approach, results comparison, cost analysis (training cost vs API cost savings), and recommendations.

deliverables: Evaluation comparison: fine-tuned vs base model with prompting
Decision report: when to fine-tune vs prompt
tools: Google Colab or similar GPU access

rubric.description: Training data is clean, well-formatted, and representative of the target task. At least 100 high-quality examples.
LoRA fine-tuning completes successfully with documented hyperparameters. Training loss shows proper convergence.
Systematic comparison between fine-tuned and base model on held-out data. Multiple metrics reported.
Clear, honest assessment of whether fine-tuning was worth it for this task, with cost and quality tradeoffs documented.
